question:
A-2. Let \( a, b, c \) be the lengths of the sides of a triangle, let \( p=(a+b+c) / 2 \), and \( r \) be the radius of the inscribed cricle. Show that
\[
\frac{1}{(p-a)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(p-b)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(p-c)^{2}} \geqq \frac{1}{r^{2}}
\]
solution:
A-2 The area of the given triangle can be calculated in two ways, \( A=p r \) and \( A=\sqrt{p(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)} \). Squaring and equating we get \( p^{2} r^{2} \) \( =p(p-a)(p-b)(p-c) \). Setting \( x=1 /(p-a), y=1 /(p-b), z=1 /(p-c) \) we can write this equation in the form
\[
\frac{1}{r^{2}}=p x y z=x y z\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{z}\right) .
\]

Thus we need only show that \( y z+x z+x y \leqq x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2} \). However this follows from the trivial inequalities \( y^{2}+z^{2} \geqq 2 y z, x^{2}+z^{2} \geqq 2 x z, x^{2}+y^{2} \geqq 2 x y \).

descriptive_long.question:
A-2. Let \( sideone, sidetwo, sidethree \) be the lengths of the sides of a triangle, let \( semiper=(sideone+sidetwo+sidethree) / 2 \), and \( inradius \) be the radius of the inscribed cricle. Show that
\[
\frac{1}{(semiper-sideone)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(semiper-sidetwo)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(semiper-sidethree)^{2}} \geqq \frac{1}{inradius^{2}}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
A-2 The area of the given triangle can be calculated in two ways, \( area=semiper\,inradius \) and \( area=\sqrt{semiper(semiper-sideone)(semiper-sidetwo)(semiper-sidethree)} \). Squaring and equating we get \( semiper^{2} inradius^{2}=semiper(semiper-sideone)(semiper-sidetwo)(semiper-sidethree) \). Setting \( recipone=1 /(semiper-sideone), reciptwo=1 /(semiper-sidetwo), recipthree=1 /(semiper-sidethree) \) we can write this equation in the form
\[
\frac{1}{inradius^{2}}=semiper\,recipone\,reciptwo\,recipthree=recipone reciptwo recipthree\left(\frac{1}{recipone}+\frac{1}{reciptwo}+\frac{1}{recipthree}\right) .
\]

Thus we need only show that \( reciptwo\,recipthree+recipone\,recipthree+recipone\,reciptwo \leqq recipone^{2}+reciptwo^{2}+recipthree^{2} \). However this follows from the trivial inequalities \( reciptwo^{2}+recipthree^{2} \geqq 2\,reciptwo\,recipthree, recipone^{2}+recipthree^{2} \geqq 2\,recipone\,recipthree, recipone^{2}+reciptwo^{2} \geqq 2\,recipone\,reciptwo \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A-2. Let \( orchardry, buttercup, cliffside \) be the lengths of the sides of a triangle, let \( honeycomb=(orchardry+buttercup+cliffside) / 2 \), and \( meadowlark \) be the radius of the inscribed cricle. Show that
\[
\frac{1}{(honeycomb-orchardry)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(honeycomb-buttercup)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(honeycomb-cliffside)^{2}} \geqq \frac{1}{meadowlark^{2}}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-2 The area of the given triangle can be calculated in two ways, \( driftwood=honeycomb meadowlark \) and \( driftwood=\sqrt{honeycomb(honeycomb-orchardry)(honeycomb-buttercup)(honeycomb-cliffside)} \). Squaring and equating we get \( honeycomb^{2} meadowlark^{2} \) \( =honeycomb(honeycomb-orchardry)(honeycomb-buttercup)(honeycomb-cliffside) \). Setting \( raincloud=1 /(honeycomb-orchardry), windchimes=1 /(honeycomb-buttercup), stargazer=1 /(honeycomb-cliffside) \) we can write this equation in the form
\[
\frac{1}{meadowlark^{2}}=honeycomb raincloud windchimes stargazer=raincloud windchimes stargazer\left(\frac{1}{raincloud}+\frac{1}{windchimes}+\frac{1}{stargazer}\right) .
\]

Thus we need only show that \( windchimes stargazer+raincloud stargazer+raincloud windchimes \leqq raincloud^{2}+windchimes^{2}+stargazer^{2} \). However this follows from the trivial inequalities \( windchimes^{2}+stargazer^{2} \geqq 2 windchimes stargazer, raincloud^{2}+stargazer^{2} \geqq 2 raincloud stargazer, raincloud^{2}+windchimes^{2} \geqq 2 raincloud windchimes \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A-2. Let \( innerangleone, innerangletwo, inneranglethree \) be the lengths of the sides of a triangle, let \( semiarea=(innerangleone+innerangletwo+inneranglethree) / 2 \), and \( outerradius \) be the radius of the inscribed cricle. Show that
\[
\frac{1}{(semiarea-innerangleone)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(semiarea-innerangletwo)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(semiarea-inneranglethree)^{2}} \geqq \frac{1}{outerradius^{2}}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-2 The area of the given triangle can be calculated in two ways, \( perimeter=semiarea outerradius \) and \( perimeter=\sqrt{semiarea(semiarea-innerangleone)(semiarea-innerangletwo)(semiarea-inneranglethree)} \). Squaring and equating we get \( semiarea^{2} outerradius^{2}=semiarea(semiarea-innerangleone)(semiarea-innerangletwo)(semiarea-inneranglethree) \). Setting \( knownvalue=1 /(semiarea-innerangleone), fixednumber=1 /(semiarea-innerangletwo), certainconst=1 /(semiarea-inneranglethree) \) we can write this equation in the form
\[
\frac{1}{outerradius^{2}}=semiarea knownvalue fixednumber certainconst=knownvalue fixednumber certainconst\left(\frac{1}{knownvalue}+\frac{1}{fixednumber}+\frac{1}{certainconst}\right) .
\]

Thus we need only show that \( fixednumber certainconst+knownvalue certainconst+knownvalue fixednumber \leqq knownvalue^{2}+fixednumber^{2}+certainconst^{2} \). However this follows from the trivial inequalities \( fixednumber^{2}+certainconst^{2} \geqq 2 fixednumber certainconst, knownvalue^{2}+certainconst^{2} \geqq 2 knownvalue certainconst, knownvalue^{2}+fixednumber^{2} \geqq 2 knownvalue fixednumber \).

garbled_string.question:
A-2. Let \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mnbvcxzi \) be the lengths of the sides of a triangle, let \( lkjhgfds=(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+mnbvcxzi) / 2 \), and \( poiuytre \) be the radius of the inscribed cricle. Show that
\[
\frac{1}{(lkjhgfds-qzxwvtnp)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(lkjhgfds-hjgrksla)^{2}}+\frac{1}{(lkjhgfds-mnbvcxzi)^{2}} \geqq \frac{1}{poiuytre^{2}}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
A-2 The area of the given triangle can be calculated in two ways, \( asdfghjk=lkjhgfds\,poiuytre \) and \( asdfghjk=\sqrt{lkjhgfds(lkjhgfds-qzxwvtnp)(lkjhgfds-hjgrksla)(lkjhgfds-mnbvcxzi)} \). Squaring and equating we get \( lkjhgfds^{2} poiuytre^{2}=lkjhgfds(lkjhgfds-qzxwvtnp)(lkjhgfds-hjgrksla)(lkjhgfds-mnbvcxzi) \). Setting \( zxcvbnma=1 /(lkjhgfds-qzxwvtnp), qwertyui=1 /(lkjhgfds-hjgrksla), plmoknij=1 /(lkjhgfds-mnbvcxzi) \) we can write this equation in the form
\[
\frac{1}{poiuytre^{2}}=lkjhgfds\,zxcvbnma\,qwertyui\,plmoknij=zxcvbnma\,qwertyui\,plmoknij\left(\frac{1}{zxcvbnma}+\frac{1}{qwertyui}+\frac{1}{plmoknij}\right) .
\]

Thus we need only show that \( qwertyui\,plmoknij+zxcvbnma\,plmoknij+zxcvbnma\,qwertyui \leqq zxcvbnma^{2}+qwertyui^{2}+plmoknij^{2} \). However this follows from the trivial inequalities \( qwertyui^{2}+plmoknij^{2} \geqq 2 qwertyui\,plmoknij, zxcvbnma^{2}+plmoknij^{2} \geqq 2 zxcvbnma\,plmoknij, zxcvbnma^{2}+qwertyui^{2} \geqq 2 zxcvbnma\,qwertyui \).

kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  From geometry to an algebraic inequality  
Because $ABCD$ is tangential, Pitot's theorem gives
\[
a+c=b+d. \tag{1}
\]
Combining (1) with $a+b+c+d=2p$ yields
\[
a+c=b+d=p. \tag{2}
\]
Hence
\[
p-a=c,\quad p-c=a,\quad p-b=d,\quad p-d=b. \tag{3}
\]

Let $\Delta$ be the area of $ABCD$.  
For every bicentric quadrilateral both Brahmagupta's formula and the incircle-area relation hold:
\[
\Delta^{2}=(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)(p-d),\qquad 
\Delta=pr .
\]
Consequently
\[
r^{2}= \frac{(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)(p-d)}{p^{2}}. \tag{4}
\]

Introduce the positive variables
\[
x=\frac{1}{p-a},\qquad y=\frac{1}{p-b},\qquad 
z=\frac{1}{p-c},\qquad w=\frac{1}{p-d}. \tag{5}
\]
With (3) we have
\[
\frac{1}{x}=c,\quad \frac{1}{z}=a,\quad 
\frac{1}{y}=d,\quad \frac{1}{w}=b,
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{w}=p. \tag{6}
\]

Equation (4) rewrites, using (5)-(6), as
\[
\frac{1}{r^{2}}=p^{2}xyzw
             =(xyzw)\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{x}+\tfrac{1}{z}\Bigr)^{2}.
\]
Thus (\*) is equivalent to the purely algebraic statement

For all positive $x,y,z,w$ satisfying
\[
\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{w}, \tag{7}
\]
one has
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+w^{2}\;\ge\;
(xyzw)\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{x}+\tfrac{1}{z}\Bigr)^{2}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 2.  Proof of the algebraic inequality  
Put
\[
S=\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{w}>0,\qquad
A=xz,\qquad B=yw. \tag{9}
\]
Then
\[
x+z=SA,\qquad y+w=SB,\qquad xyzw=AB. \tag{10}
\]

Using $u^{2}+v^{2}\ge\dfrac{(u+v)^{2}}{2}$ (Cauchy or Jensen) we get
\[
x^{2}+z^{2}\ge\frac{(x+z)^{2}}{2}=\frac{S^{2}A^{2}}{2},\qquad
y^{2}+w^{2}\ge\frac{(y+w)^{2}}{2}=\frac{S^{2}B^{2}}{2}. \tag{11}
\]
Adding these inequalities yields
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+w^{2}\;\ge\;\frac{S^{2}}{2}\,(A^{2}+B^{2}). \tag{12}
\]

Because $A^{2}+B^{2}\ge 2AB$ (AM-GM), (12) implies
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+w^{2}\;\ge\;S^{2}AB
      \;=\;(xyzw)\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{x}+\tfrac{1}{z}\Bigr)^{2},
\]
which is exactly (8).

Equality in (8) requires simultaneous equality in both steps (11) and AM-GM; hence
\[
x=z,\quad y=w,\quad\text{and}\quad A=B.
\]
These three constraints force
\[
x=z=y=w. \tag{13}
\]
Conversely, $x=z=y=w$ clearly gives equality, so (13) characterises the equality case for (8).

Step 3.  Returning to geometry  
From (5) and (13) we obtain
\[
p-a=p-b=p-c=p-d \quad\Longrightarrow\quad a=b=c=d. \tag{14}
\]
Thus $ABCD$ is an equilateral cyclic quadrilateral.

In a circle, equal chords subtend equal arcs; hence the four equal sides cut the circumcircle into four congruent arcs of $90^{\circ}$.  
Because each interior angle of a cyclic quadrilateral equals half of its intercepted arc, every interior angle of $ABCD$ is
\[
\frac{1}{2}\times180^{\circ}=90^{\circ}.
\]
Therefore $ABCD$ is a square.

Consequently inequality (\*) is valid for every bicentric quadrilateral, and equality occurs if and only if $ABCD$ is a square. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension / more variables  
   – The triangle problem involves three variables; the present problem
     involves four independent variables, increasing both algebraic
     and geometric complexity.

2. Additional structural assumptions  
   – The figure must be simultaneously tangential and cyclic
     (bicentric), forcing the solver to bring together Brahmagupta’s
     formula, Pitot’s theorem, and cyclic-quadrilateral properties.

3. Deeper theoretical tools  
   – The solution needs homogenisation, normalisation, and a genuine
     multivariable optimisation (via Lagrange multipliers) on a manifold
     with two independent constraints, far beyond the single
     elementary inequality that finishes the original triangle task.

4. Longer logical chain  
   – Derivation (area identities → algebraic translation → homogeneous
     reduction → constrained minimisation → geometric back-translation)
     is appreciably longer than the single-page resolution of the
     original problem.

5. Strict classification of equality  
   – Besides proving the inequality, the enhanced variant demands a
     characterization of all equality cases, adding an extra layer of
     reasoning.

Collectively these additions render the enhanced kernel variant
substantially harder than both the original problem and the simpler
kernel variant that merely restated the triangle inequality.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  From geometry to an algebraic inequality  
Because $ABCD$ is tangential, Pitot's theorem gives
\[
a+c=b+d. \tag{1}
\]
Combining (1) with $a+b+c+d=2p$ yields
\[
a+c=b+d=p. \tag{2}
\]
Hence
\[
p-a=c,\quad p-c=a,\quad p-b=d,\quad p-d=b. \tag{3}
\]

Let $\Delta$ be the area of $ABCD$.  
For every bicentric quadrilateral both Brahmagupta's formula and the incircle-area relation hold:
\[
\Delta^{2}=(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)(p-d),\qquad 
\Delta=pr .
\]
Consequently
\[
r^{2}= \frac{(p-a)(p-b)(p-c)(p-d)}{p^{2}}. \tag{4}
\]

Introduce the positive variables
\[
x=\frac{1}{p-a},\qquad y=\frac{1}{p-b},\qquad 
z=\frac{1}{p-c},\qquad w=\frac{1}{p-d}. \tag{5}
\]
With (3) we have
\[
\frac{1}{x}=c,\quad \frac{1}{z}=a,\quad 
\frac{1}{y}=d,\quad \frac{1}{w}=b,
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{w}=p. \tag{6}
\]

Equation (4) rewrites, using (5)-(6), as
\[
\frac{1}{r^{2}}=p^{2}xyzw
             =(xyzw)\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{x}+\tfrac{1}{z}\Bigr)^{2}.
\]
Thus (\*) is equivalent to the purely algebraic statement

For all positive $x,y,z,w$ satisfying
\[
\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{w}, \tag{7}
\]
one has
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+w^{2}\;\ge\;
(xyzw)\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{x}+\tfrac{1}{z}\Bigr)^{2}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 2.  Proof of the algebraic inequality  
Put
\[
S=\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{w}>0,\qquad
A=xz,\qquad B=yw. \tag{9}
\]
Then
\[
x+z=SA,\qquad y+w=SB,\qquad xyzw=AB. \tag{10}
\]

Using $u^{2}+v^{2}\ge\dfrac{(u+v)^{2}}{2}$ (Cauchy or Jensen) we get
\[
x^{2}+z^{2}\ge\frac{(x+z)^{2}}{2}=\frac{S^{2}A^{2}}{2},\qquad
y^{2}+w^{2}\ge\frac{(y+w)^{2}}{2}=\frac{S^{2}B^{2}}{2}. \tag{11}
\]
Adding these inequalities yields
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+w^{2}\;\ge\;\frac{S^{2}}{2}\,(A^{2}+B^{2}). \tag{12}
\]

Because $A^{2}+B^{2}\ge 2AB$ (AM-GM), (12) implies
\[
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+w^{2}\;\ge\;S^{2}AB
      \;=\;(xyzw)\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{x}+\tfrac{1}{z}\Bigr)^{2},
\]
which is exactly (8).

Equality in (8) requires simultaneous equality in both steps (11) and AM-GM; hence
\[
x=z,\quad y=w,\quad\text{and}\quad A=B.
\]
These three constraints force
\[
x=z=y=w. \tag{13}
\]
Conversely, $x=z=y=w$ clearly gives equality, so (13) characterises the equality case for (8).

Step 3.  Returning to geometry  
From (5) and (13) we obtain
\[
p-a=p-b=p-c=p-d \quad\Longrightarrow\quad a=b=c=d. \tag{14}
\]
Thus $ABCD$ is an equilateral cyclic quadrilateral.

In a circle, equal chords subtend equal arcs; hence the four equal sides cut the circumcircle into four congruent arcs of $90^{\circ}$.  
Because each interior angle of a cyclic quadrilateral equals half of its intercepted arc, every interior angle of $ABCD$ is
\[
\frac{1}{2}\times180^{\circ}=90^{\circ}.
\]
Therefore $ABCD$ is a square.

Consequently inequality (\*) is valid for every bicentric quadrilateral, and equality occurs if and only if $ABCD$ is a square. \hfill$\square$
